cleaned things up a bit so it wasn't such a mess
Finally, ThinkPHP also supports loading multiple resources at once. Say I want to load Bootstrap's JS along with some other JS files — I can write it like this:

The thing is, I find this kind of annoying, because this syntax can't have any line breaks or spaces. If you add a line break or a space, the import won't work — keep that in mind. So written this way, you can pull in all your JS files in one go.

In this post we learned how to import third-party resources into the ThinkPHP framework. That means later on, whenever we want to prettify things, we can lean on Bootstrap really easily — just declare
at the very top of the HTML and let ThinkPHP handle the rest. Next time I'm planning to cover ThinkPHP's built-in tags.

It's finally time to bring in some third-party resources. In this post I'll pair Bootstrap with the Layout we built last time, so you can clearly see the difference between a layout that loaded correctly and one that didn't. I'll walk through where to drop the files, how to link them, and the shorthand tags ThinkPHP gives you for loading CSS and JS.
